interactions.summary: Jiang explains why power prefers scientific individualism: it creates responsibility, makes people work harder, and blocks collective action by locating problems inside the individual.
Students briefly respond to the two-worldview comparison and ask whether experience can include other people's perspectives.
Jiang answers by saying the second system's authority is science and psychiatry, which he claims teaches dependence on experts rather than ordinary supports such as walking, rest, and friends.

speaker_notes.note: Segment is empty in the clean transcript after boundary repair.

claims.claim: Science functions as a religion in the modern world because it supplies the authority structure that people are taught to trust.
The individual-responsibility worldview benefits power because it makes people work harder and converts their labor into real wealth for powerful people.
A worldview that locates all problems inside the individual prevents collective identification and collective action.
Psychiatry and psychology are framed as authority systems that can deepen dependence rather than solve sadness.

signature_moments.moment: If your problems are only inside you, you cannot join with anyone else to change the world.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you are incapable of collective action
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the political hinge of the polytheism/science contrast.

signature_moments.moment: The authority system converts ordinary sadness into professional dependence.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the system is not designed to cure you
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It shows how Jiang links personal distress to institutional power rather than private pathology.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The only way to change the world in Jiang's model; modern individualism prevents it by privatizing problems.
Science treated not as neutral method but as the modern authority system people obey.

uncertainty_notes.note: The psychiatry claim is provocative and medically sensitive; public read should present it as Jiang's argument, not as medical advice.
